      render: function (rm, oControl) {
        rm.openStart(oControl.getTag(), oControl);       // <peppie-tile id="__control0">
        rm.style("display", "block");
        rm.openEnd();
        rm.close(oControl.getTag());
      }
    },
    onAfterRendering: function () {
      var oDom = this.getDomRef();
      if (oDom) { oDom.data = this.getData(); }          // property, not attribute (pitfall #1)
    },
    setData: function (vData) {
      this.setProperty("data", vData, true /* suppress re-render */);
      var oDom = this.getDomRef();
      if (oDom) { oDom.data = vData; }                   // live update in place (pitfall #3)
      return this;
    }
  });
});

rm.openStart(tag, oControl) is the trick in the renderer: passing the control writes UI5's id and bookkeeping onto the custom element, so apiVersion: 2 can patch it in place instead of throwing it away on every change.

Wiring it into a real UI5 app

Four small pieces, all of them standard UI5.

1. Bootstrap — load the custom elements before UI5, register a resource root for your control namespace, and pick a theme:

<!-- index.html -->
<script src="webcomponents.js"></script>            <!-- define elements first (pitfall #4) -->
<script id="sap-ui-bootstrap"
  src="https://sdk.openui5.org/resources/sap-ui-core.js"
  data-sap-ui-theme="sap_horizon_dark"
  data-sap-ui-libs="sap.m,sap.ui.layout"
  data-sap-ui-resourceroots='{"peppie": "./peppie/"}'
  data-sap-ui-oninit="module:sap/ui/core/ComponentSupport"
  data-sap-ui-compatVersion="edge"
  data-sap-ui-async="true"></script>

2. The model — declared in the manifest, so it's there before the view:

"sap.ui5": {
  "models": {
    "": { "type": "sap.ui.model.json.JSONModel", "uri": "model/data.json" }
  }
}

3. The view — the namespace peppie.control resolves to peppie/control/WebComponent.js via the resource root. Now binding is exactly what you'd hope for:

<mvc:View controllerName="peppie.controller.Main"
  xmlns:mvc="sap.ui.core.mvc" xmlns="sap.m"
  xmlns:grid="sap.ui.layout.cssgrid" xmlns:wc="peppie.control">

  <VBox class="peppieWrap">

    <!-- a native UI5 control, bound to the same model -->
    <MessageStrip text="{/suggest}" type="{/suggestType}" showIcon="true"/>

    <!-- tiles: one control, four bindings, in a real CSS grid -->
    <grid:CSSGrid gridTemplateColumns="repeat(auto-fit, minmax(220px, 1fr))" gridGap="0.6rem">
      <wc:WebComponent tag="peppie-tile" data="{/tiles/0}"/>
      <wc:WebComponent tag="peppie-tile" data="{/tiles/1}"/>
      <wc:WebComponent tag="peppie-tile" data="{/tiles/2}"/>
      <wc:WebComponent tag="peppie-tile" data="{/tiles/3}"/>
    </grid:CSSGrid>

    <!-- cards: two columns that each stack tightly -->
    <HBox wrap="Wrap" class="peppieCols">
      <VBox class="peppieCol">
        <wc:WebComponent tag="peppie-flow"        data="{/flow}"/>
        <wc:WebComponent tag="peppie-metric-card" data="{/p1}"/>
        <!-- … taycan, ring -->
      </VBox>
      <VBox class="peppieCol">
        <wc:WebComponent tag="peppie-metric-card" data="{/solar}"/>
        <wc:WebComponent tag="peppie-metric-card" data="{/wp}"/>
        <!-- … tesla, hue -->
      </VBox>
    </HBox>

  </VBox>
</mvc:View>

One generic control wraps every element in the library. Ordinary UI5 layout does the arranging — a sap.ui.layout.cssgrid.CSSGrid for the tile row and two sap.m.VBox columns in a wrapping HBox for the cards (a float-based sap.ui.layout.Grid left ragged gaps between cards of different heights, so I switched). And a native sap.m.MessageStrip sits at the top, bound to the same model — proof that stock UI5 controls and Web Components mix freely in one view.

 

Running OpenUI5 app (sap_horizon_dark). The toolbar and its buttons are stock sap.m controls; everything below is Web Components bound to a JSONModel. Two worlds, one app.

The pitfalls (this is where the time goes)

1. Property, not attribute. The big one. UI5 binding, and most snippets you'll copy, write to attributes. Web Components take rich values — objects, arrays — as properties. setAttribute("data", {...}) stringifies to [object Object]. So in onAfterRendering you do element.data = this.getData(). That one line is why this works at all.

2. Bind objects, update them immutably. The wrapper binds a whole object (data="{/tiles/0}"), and JSONModel compares by reference. Mutate that object in place and the binding never fires. The tempting fix — model.refresh(true) — works, but it's a smell: it re-evaluates every binding in the app to paper over mutated state. The clean fix is to treat model data as immutable and replace the node with a new object, so the reference changes and exactly that binding updates:

onSimulate: function () {
  var m = this._oModel, oTile = m.getProperty("/tiles/0");
  // new object → new reference → the {/tiles/0} binding fires on its own
  m.setProperty("/tiles/0", Object.assign({}, oTile, { value: solar.toFixed(1) }));
}

No refresh(true), no shared state mutated in place — each changed node fires its own binding.

3. Re-render vs. live update. UI5 controls invalidate and re-render. You do not want to tear down and rebuild a custom element on every model tick. That's why setData uses suppressInvalidate = true and updates the live element directly. In the demo, "Simulate reading" changes the model and every tile, bar and card updates without a single re-render.

4. Load order and the upgrade trap. Define your custom elements before the control first renders. If UI5 renders <peppie-tile> before the browser knows the class, it's an inert unknown element; worse, setting .data on a not-yet-upgraded element creates an own-property that shadows the class setter after upgrade. Loading webcomponents.js in <head> before the bootstrap avoids the whole class of "it works on refresh but not first load" bugs.

5. Layout and height. Web Components are display: inline by default — render them display: block (the control does this). And don't drop them into a sap.m.Page that has no height: the Page positions its content absolutely, so a zero-height container renders a blank screen. I lost a minute to exactly that; a VBox/Grid that flows with content, or a container with a real height, fixes it.

6. Getting events back into UI5. Binding flows data in; for interaction out (a light toggled, a card clicked) let the element dispatch a CustomEvent and re-fire it as a UI5 event:

metadata: { events: { action: {} } },
onAfterRendering: function () {
  var oDom = this.getDomRef();
  oDom.data = this.getData();
  if (!this._wired) {                       // wire once
    oDom.addEventListener("peppie-action", (e) => this.fireAction({ detail: e.detail }));
    this._wired = true;
  }
}

Now <wc:WebComponent action=".onAction"/> works like any UI5 event handler.

The honest alternative

If you want to stay fully inside the SAP toolbox, you can: sap.f.Card for the layout, sap.suite.ui.microchart for the little charts. You get binding for free and a Fiori-native look. For this deliberately non-Fiori design, the Web Component route was less fighting and more reuse — the same components run outside UI5 too. Pick per project; just know both exist.

Try it

The full runnable demo is in ui5-demo/ — OpenUI5 from CDN, one wrapper control, a JSONModel, no build step. npx serve ui5-demo, open the URL, hit Simulate reading and Theme.

What this shows about building with AI

I described the goal — "wrap these custom elements as a data-bound UI5 control" — and the agent produced the control and got the app running fast, naming most pitfalls as we hit them: the attribute-vs-property gotcha, the sap.m.Page height trap, the upgrade-order bug. But not everything it produced was senior-grade. Its first fix for the object-binding problem was model.refresh(true) — and my own instinct said mutating model state and force-refreshing is not done. We replaced it with immutable setProperty updates. That's the real division of labour: the agent brings speed and breadth; you bring the judgement that turns working into clean. Treat its output as a strong first draft, not the final commit.

Your turn — a give-away

This demo is JavaScript. It should be TypeScript: the model typed, the control's managed properties typed, the Web Components' data typed end to end. That's a real, multi-file refactor across the UI5 Tooling — exactly the kind of well-scoped job an agent is good at and you review.

I ran this prompt myself before publishing it, and it turned out to be the whole series' thesis in one artifact. Left vague, the agent produces something that looks converted but doesn't build: a broken generator dependency (@ui5/ts-interface-generator pulls in an ESM-only yargs and won't run), the OpenUI5 framework config the CDN app never had, a setData signature that clashes with the generated interface, a type-only module that gets stripped to a non-module. So the prompt below is deliberately specific — exact versions, and the three real blockers baked in. That specificity is the difference between "the agent tried" and the build is green.

Run it in the ui5-demo/ folder and hold it to the acceptance criteria at the end.

You are a senior SAPUI5 + TypeScript engineer. Convert the JavaScript UI5 app in this folder to
TypeScript — fully typed, strict:true — and make it BUILD and SERVE with the UI5 Tooling. Do NOT
change runtime behaviour; the app must render and behave exactly as it does now. Work in small,
reviewable steps: layout & framework first, then toolchain, then types, then module conversion.

0. Environment. @ui5/cli 4 wants Node >= 22.12; older Node only prints an EBADENGINE warning and
   still works.

1. Layout & framework — the app currently loads OpenUI5 from a CDN; that must change, because the
   UI5 Tooling serves the framework itself.
   - Move all app sources under webapp/: index.html, Component.ts, manifest.json, controller/,
     view/, control/, model/, and the web-components module.
   - Add ui5.yaml that pins OpenUI5 locally (no CDN):
       specVersion: "3.0"
       metadata: { name: peppie }
       type: application
       framework:
         name: OpenUI5
         version: "1.120.46"
         libraries:
           - name: sap.ui.core
           - name: sap.m
           - name: sap.ui.layout
           - name: themelib_sap_horizon      # required for sap_horizon_dark
       builder:
         customTasks:
           - name: ui5-tooling-transpile-task
             afterTask: replaceVersion
       server:
         customMiddleware:
           - name: ui5-tooling-transpile-middleware
             afterMiddleware: compression
   - In webapp/index.html change the bootstrap src from the CDN URL to "resources/sap-ui-core.js"
     (served by the Tooling). Keep data-sap-ui-theme="sap_horizon_dark", the ComponentSupport
     oninit, and data-sap-ui-resourceroots='{"peppie": "./"}'. Ensure manifest.json sap.app.id =
     "peppie" so the resourceroots, the /** @namespace peppie.* */ values, and the manifest id all
     agree.

2. Toolchain & exact, known-good versions.
   devDependencies:
     "@openui5/types": "1.120.46"
     "@ui5/cli": "^4.0.57"
     "@ui5/ts-interface-generator": "^0.11.1"
     "typescript": "^5.9.3"
     "ui5-tooling-transpile": "^3.11.3"
   overrides (MANDATORY — see step 4):
     "@ui5/ts-interface-generator": { "yargs": "^17.7.2" }
   scripts:
     "ts:check": "tsc --noEmit"
     "build": "ui5 build --clean-dest"
     "start": "ui5 serve --open index.html"
   tsconfig.json: strict:true, target/module "ES2022", moduleResolution "node",
     lib ["ES2022","DOM","DOM.Iterable"], skipLibCheck:true, rootDir "webapp",
     paths { "peppie/*": ["webapp/*"] }, types ["@openui5/types"],
     include ["webapp/**/*.ts","webapp/**/*.d.ts"].

3. Convert each sap.ui.define([...], function(){}) into an ES-module TypeScript class with
   import / export default, UI5 TS style, keeping the namespace via /** @namespace peppie.control */.

4. The custom control — mind the interface generator.
   - Keep the metadata block; type the renderer (RenderManager), onAfterRendering, and the setData
     override.
   - Run @ui5/ts-interface-generator to emit WebComponent.gen.d.ts (typed getTag/setTag/
     getData/setData plus $WebComponentSettings), and make sure tsconfig includes it.
   - BLOCKER: @ui5/ts-interface-generator@0.11.1 require()s yargs but resolves the ESM-only
     yargs@18 -> ERR_REQUIRE_ESM, so the generator won't run at all. The overrides pin in step 2
     (yargs ^17.7.2) fixes it. Install that BEFORE running the generator.
   - Two things the generator will NOT do for you:
       a) It prints three constructor(...) overload lines and says "copy & paste manually" — paste
          them into the class (referencing $WebComponentSettings). It never edits your source.
       b) It also generates setData(data: object | null): this. Your hand-written setData MUST use
          the exact same `object | null` parameter type, or declaration-merging fails with an
          overload-compatibility error.

5. Types. Add webapp/model/types.ts with interfaces TileData, FlowRow, FlowData, MetricItem,
   MetricCardData, EvCardData, RingCam, RingCardData, LightGroup, LightsCardData, and a root
   DashboardModel including suggest:string and
   suggestType:"Success"|"Warning"|"Error"|"Information"|"None". Type the JSONModel and the
   controller's onSimulate against DashboardModel.

6. The web components — make it a real UI5 module.
   - Write webcomponents.ts with a typed `set data(d: …)` per element and augment
     HTMLElementTagNameMap so createElement("peppie-tile") and the control's getDomRef() are typed.
   - IMPORTANT: add at least one runtime `export` (e.g. export { PeppieTile, PeppieFlow, … }). A
     module with only TYPE exports gets type-stripped to a plain script and is NOT wrapped as a
     sap.ui.define AMD module — the build then warns it "requires eval". A real runtime export
     makes it a proper module.
   - Have Component.ts do a side-effect import "./webcomponents"; so the custom elements are defined
     before the controls render.
   - In the wrapper, type the element so assigning .data is checked. A single, commented
     `getDomRef() as HTMLElement & { data: unknown }` cast is fine — do not use `any`.

Acceptance criteria (all must pass):
- `npm run ts:check` -> zero errors with strict:true.
- `npm run build` (ui5 build) -> green, no warnings, produces a runnable build.
- `npm start` (ui5 serve) -> renders identically: four tiles, two-column cards, the MessageStrip,
  and "Simulate reading" flipping the strip between Warning and Success.
- The generic WebComponent wrapper still wraps any element through one `data` property; no `any`
  anywhere (the one commented getDomRef() cast excepted).

When done, show me the final tsconfig.json, ui5.yaml, package.json, the typed WebComponent, and
model/types.ts.

Validated: this produced tsc --noEmit with zero strict errors, a green ui5 build, and a ui5 serve render pixel-identical to the JavaScript original. First draft from the agent; the specificity — and the final commit — from you.

Next

The house is connected (Part 1) and it has a real UI5 frontend (Part 2). In Part 3 I pull back the curtain on how most of this actually got built: an AI agent that read a login captcha, hacked a solar inverter, and then deleted its own hack when the clean route opened up.

Part 3 — I let an AI agent connect the whole house — is next.

entry.title: Extending the Fiori App Display Supplier List (F1861): A Practical Approach Using CDS Views
entry.content:
Introduction

Extending standard SAP Fiori applications is a common requirement in S/4HANA projects.

In a recent scenario, I needed to include additional supplier fields from table LFA1 into the standard app Display Supplier List (F1861). However, even though the fields existed in the backend, they were not available in the Fiori application.

The Challenge

At first glance, extending a standard app may seem straightforward, but when dealing with CDS-based applications, simply adding fields at database level is not enough.

The F1861 app relies on CDS views and requires specific extension steps to properly expose additional fields.

SAP Note

According to SAP Note 3200905, extending standard CDS-based applications requires following a specific approach to ensure that custom fields are correctly exposed in both UI and analytical layers.

3200905 - Display Supplier List: Standard extensibility delivered by View Entity

Approach Overview

To address this, the following approach was implemented:

  • Extend the CDS view used as data source (C_Supplier)
  • Extend the consumption CDS view (C_SupplierList)
  • Apply UI annotations via Metadata Extension

Implementation

The standard interface view C_Supplier was extended to include custom fields from table LFA1.

extend view C_Supplier with ZEXT_C_SUPPLIER
  association [1..1] to lfa1 as _Supplier on $projection.supplier = _Supplier.lifnr
{
  _Supplier.ort02                                         as ExtDistrictName,
  _Supplier.brsch                                         as ExtIndustry,
  cast( _Supplier.gbdat as zext_gbdat preserving type )   as ExtBirthDate, // Custom data element used – standard conversion exit PDATE is not supported in CDS
  _Supplier.txjcd                                         as ExtTaxJurisdiction,
  _Supplier.regss                                         as SupplIsSocialInsuranceRegtrd,
  _Supplier.actss                                         as SocialInsuranceActivityCode,
  _Supplier.cnae                                          as ExtTaxEconomicActivityCode
}

A custom data element was created for the birth date field, as the standard conversion exit (PDATE) is not supported in CDS views. This allowed proper handling of the field without relying on conversion exits.

Next, the consumption view C_SupplierList was extended:

extend view C_SupplierList with ZEXT_C_SUPPLIERLIST
{
  cast( '' as ort02_gp )                         as ExtDistrictName,
  cast( '' as brsch )                            as ExtIndustry,
  cast( '' as zext_gbdat )                       as ExtBirthDate,
  cast( '' as txjcd )                            as ExtTaxJurisdiction,
  cast( '' as j_1aregss preserving type )        as SupplIsSocialInsuranceRegtrd,
  cast( '' as j_1aactss )                        as SocialInsuranceActivityCode,
  cast( '' as j_1bcnae )                         as ExtTaxEconomicActivityCode
}

A metadata extension was then used to control the visibility of fields in the UI:

@Metadata.layer: #CORE
annotate view C_SupplierList with
{
  @UI: { hidden: true }
  BPTaxType;

  @UI: { hidden: true }
  VATRegistration;

  @UI: { hidden: true }
  TaxTypeName;
}

This approach ensures that the extension remains upgrade-safe and aligned with SAP extensibility guidelines.

Result

After the extensions and annotations, the custom fields were successfully displayed in the F1861 app.

Display Supplier List (new fields)

Challenges Faced

Some challenges were encountered during the implementation:

  • Fields not appearing due to missing annotations
  • Issues with CDS associations
  • Inconsistent behavior when extending standard views
  • Limitations when reusing associations locally

Key Learnings

This scenario highlights several important aspects when working with SAP extensibility:

  • Understanding the CDS view hierarchy is essential
  • Metadata Extensions are required for UI exposure
  • SAP Notes often contain critical implementation details

Final Thoughts

This scenario demonstrates how standard SAP applications can be extended in a structured way, without modifying the core system, following clean core principles.

I would be interested to hear how other teams are handling Fiori extensibility scenarios.

👉Feel free to share your approach or exchange ideas.

entry.title: Media Handling in SAP CAP: Mixing Static and Uploaded Images in Fiori Elements V4
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

Introduction

When building applications with the SAP Cloud Application Programming (CAP) model and SAP Fiori Elements V4, handling media files is a common requirement. Usually, it is straightforward: first define a LargeBinary field, and the framework generates an upload control.

However, things get complicated when we need a single table column to dynamically display images from two different sources:

  1. Static default images (loaded via CSV during deployment).

  2. Custom images uploaded by users via the UI.

This guide covers the complete end-to-end setup to achieve this:

  • Data Modeling — Defining the schema with a "Traffic Cop" virtual field.

  • UI Annotations — Exposing the right fields to the List Report and Object Page.

  • Backend Logic — Intercepting OData V4 requests in Node.js to bypass UI optimizations.

  • HANA Deployment — Resolving common driver version conflicts during deployment.

Why I Wrote This

Recently, I was developing an application on the SAP Business Technology Platform where users needed to browse a catalog of items. Some items had static placeholder images mapped to local CSV paths, while others had high-res photos explicitly uploaded by users.

When I attempted to route these dynamically using a virtual field, my Fiori V4 List Report stubbornly displayed blank "No Image" placeholders. After hours of debugging the OData V4 network payload, I realized Fiori was optimizing my request by stripping out the hidden columns my backend logic relied on. I couldn't find a definitive guide covering this specific Fiori V4 optimization trap, so I'm sharing the bulletproof workaround I built.

 

First, Let's Understand the Fiori V4 Optimization Trap

Before looking at the code, it helps to understand why the standard approach fails in Fiori Elements V4.

Fiori V4 is aggressively optimized for bandwidth. When it loads a List Report, the OData $select query only asks for the columns explicitly visible on the screen. If the backend relies on a virtual field (e.g., displayImageUrl) that calculates its value based on hidden fields (like mediaType or ImageUrl), those hidden fields arrive at the backend handler as undefined. We cannot calculate a dynamic URL if the framework refuses to fetch the underlying data.

To fix this, we don't fight the UI. Instead, we let Fiori request whatever it wants, and we use a custom CAP backend hook to silently query the database for the missing fields before delivering the final payload to the browser.

 

Architecture Overview

This solution relies on a clean separation between the database schema, the UI annotations, and the backend Node.js service.

project-root/
│
├── db/
│   ├── data/
│   │   └── mediahandling.db-Image.csv  ← Initial data linking to static image paths
│   └── schema.cds                      ← Defines the binary, static, and virtual fields
│
├── app/
│   ├── mediahandling/                  ← Your Fiori Elements application
│   │   └── webapp/
│   │       └── images/                 
│   │           ├── image1.jpg          ← The actual static placeholder files
│   │           └── image2.jpg
│   └── annotations.cds                 ← Maps the UI controls for Fiori Elements
│
└── srv/
    ├── catalogService.cds              ← Exposes the OData service
    └── catalogService.js               ← Contains the custom 'READ' interceptor logic

 

Step 1: The Data Model (schema.cds)

We need an entity that stores both the binary file and the static URL, alongside a virtual field to decide which one to render.

Critical Detail: Ensure the @ui.IsImageURL: true annotation is applied directly to the virtual field here in the schema. This instructs Fiori V4 to natively allocate an avatar control in the List Report.

namespace mediahandling.db;
using { cuid, managed } from '@sap/cds/common';

entity Image: cuid, managed {
    Name        : String(100);
    Description : String(255);
    
    // Holds the static CSV path (e.g., 'images/default.jpg')
    ImageUrl    : String; 

    // The actual uploaded file data
    @Core.MediaType  : mediaType
    image       : LargeBinary;

    // The file extension/type (e.g., 'image/jpeg')
    @Core.IsMediaType: true
    mediaType   : String;

    // The "Traffic Cop" virtual field for Fiori UI display
    @ui.IsImageURL: true
    virtual displayImageUrl : String;
}

 

Step 2: Initializing the Mock Data (CSV)

To test this, we need to load initial records into the database. Create a file named mediahandling.db-Image.csv inside the db/data/ folder.

ID;Name;Description;ImageUrl
22222222-2222-2222-2222-222222222201;Sunset Boulevard;A beautiful vibrant sunset over the city boulevard.;images/image1.jpg
22222222-2222-2222-2222-222222222202;Mountain Peak;The highest snow-capped peak in the northern mountain range.;images/image2.jpg

 

Step 3: UI Annotations (annotations.cds)

In the UI annotations, we expose the virtual displayImageUrl field in the UI.LineItem (List Report) so the dynamic image renders in the table. We expose the actual image (LargeBinary) field inside the UI.FieldGroup (Object Page) so the user gets a file upload prompt when creating or editing a record.

using MyService as service from '../../srv/catalogService';

annotate service.Image with @(
    UI.FieldGroup #GeneratedGroup : {
        $Type : 'UI.FieldGroupType',
        Data : [
            { $Type : 'UI.DataField', Label : 'Name', Value : Name },
            { $Type : 'UI.DataField', Label : 'Description', Value : Description },
            { $Type : 'UI.DataField', Label : 'Upload Image', Value : image }
        ],
    },
    UI.Facets : [
        {
            $Type : 'UI.ReferenceFacet',
            ID : 'GeneratedFacet1',
            Label : 'General Information',
            Target : '@UI.FieldGroup#GeneratedGroup',
        },
    ],
    UI.LineItem : [
        { $Type : 'UI.DataField', Label : 'Name', Value : Name },
        { $Type : 'UI.DataField', Label : 'Image', Value : displayImageUrl }, 
        { $Type : 'UI.DataField', Label : 'Description', Value : Description }
    ],
    UI.HeaderInfo:{
        $Type : 'UI.HeaderInfoType',
        TypeName : 'Image Record',
        TypeNamePlural : 'Image Records',
        Title : { $Type: 'UI.DataField', Value: ID },
        Description : { $Type: 'UI.DataField', Value: Name },
        ImageUrl : displayImageUrl 
    }
);

 

Step 4: The Backend Interceptor (catalogService.js)

This is where the magic happens. We intercept the after('READ') event. If we detect that Fiori stripped our necessary columns (mediaType and ImageUrl), we immediately execute a lightweight database query to fetch them for the visible rows. Finally, we route the URL accordingly.

const cds = require('@sap/cds');

module.exports = cds.service.impl(async function() {
    // Dynamically grab the actual service base path (e.g., /odata/v4/my)
    const srvPath = this.path;

    this.after('READ', 'Image', async (data, req) => {
        const images = Array.isArray(data) ? data : [data];
        if (images.length === 0) return;

        // If Fiori stripped the columns to save bandwidth, we step in.
        if (images[0].mediaType === undefined && images[0].ImageUrl === undefined) {
            
            // Grab the IDs of the rows currently visible on the screen
            const ids = images.map(img => img.ID);
            
            // Query the database directly to fetch the missing fields
            const dbData = await cds.tx(req).run(
                SELECT.from(req.target)
                      .columns('ID', 'mediaType', 'ImageUrl', 'IsActiveEntity')
                      .where({ ID: { 'in': ids } })
            );

            // Stitch the missing data back into the original payload
            images.forEach(img => {
                const match = dbData.find(d => d.ID === img.ID);
                if (match) {
                    img.mediaType = match.mediaType;
                    img.ImageUrl = match.ImageUrl;
                    img.IsActiveEntity = match.IsActiveEntity;
                }
            });
        }

        // Execute the Traffic Cop logic (guaranteed to have the data)
        images.forEach(image => {
            if (image.mediaType) {
                // Route to the OData media stream for uploaded binaries
                const isActive = image.IsActiveEntity !== false;
                image.displayImageUrl = `${srvPath}/Image(ID=${image.ID},IsActiveEntity=${isActive})/image`;
            } else if (image.ImageUrl) {
                // Route to the static CSV path
                image.displayImageUrl = image.ImageUrl;
            } else {
                // Fallback placeholder
                image.displayImageUrl = 'https://dummyimage.com/200x200/cccccc/000000.png&text=No+Image';
            }
        });
    });
});

 

Output:

 

Note: Deploying to SAP HANA Cloud

When transitioning from a local SQLite setup to SAP HANA Cloud, we may encounter an NPM dependency error when starting the hybrid profile.

If the project is running @SAP/cds version 9, but the latest @cap-js/hana driver expects version 10, the standard npm install will fail with an ERESOLVE conflict. We can safely bypass this strict peer dependency check and link the driver by running the following:

npm install @cap-js/hana --legacy-peer-deps
Once installed, cds watch --profile hybrid command will connect to the HANA instance seamlessly.
 

Conclusion

By shifting the data-retrieval logic into the Node.js backend rather than relying on messy UI workarounds or forced hidden columns, we maintain the strict performance benefits of Fiori Elements V4 while delivering a seamless, dynamic user experience.

entry.title: How to Find the Right Enhancement Spot in Fiori App F3516: A Practical Validation Scenario
entry.content:
Introduction

Validations are a common requirement in SAP Fiori applications. However, identifying the correct enhancement spot for a given app can be challenging.

The Challenge

In this scenario, a validation was required in the Fiori app Manage Profit Centers (Version 2) - F3516 to ensure that the segment value followed specific business rules.

The main challenge was not implementing the validation itself, but identifying the correct enhancement point in the backend.

How the Enhancement Spot Was Identified

A practical approach I often use is to identify the technical package behind the Fiori application.

This can be done by navigating to the Fiori Apps Library, checking the Implementation Information section, and identifying the OData service used by the application.

From there, the OData service can be searched in the ABAP environment (Eclipse), allowing identification of the corresponding package.

In this case, within the application package ODATA_PROFIT_CENTER_V2, an enhancement package with a very intuitive name was found:

ODATA_PROFIT_CENTER_V2_ENH (Enhancements for Manage Profit Center)

CLASS CL_FIN_PC_VALIDATION_IMPL_EX

Within this package, it was possible to identify available enhancement implementations. Among the options, the following example class was used as a reference:

class CL_FIN_PC_VALIDATION_IMPL_EX definition
  public
  final
  create public.

public section.

  interfaces IF_BADI_INTERFACE.
  interfaces IF_BADI_FIN_PC_VALIDATION.

protected section.
private section.
ENDCLASS.
CLASS CL_FIN_PC_VALIDATION_IMPL_EX IMPLEMENTATION.

  method IF_BADI_FIN_PC_VALIDATION~EXECUTE.

    data: ls_validationmessage like line of ct_messages.

    ls_validationmessage-msgid = 'FAC_MPC_PCTR'.
    ls_validationmessage-msgno = '022'.
    ls_validationmessage-msgty = 'E'.
    ls_validationmessage-msgv1 = 'The Field '.
    ls_validationmessage-msgv2 = 'Profit Center Category '.
    ls_validationmessage-msgv3 = 'contains invalid value '.

    insert ls_validationmessage into table ct_messages.

  endmethod.

ENDCLASS.

Implementation

The validation logic was implemented to ensure that segment values starting with "0" are not allowed:

METHOD if_badi_fin_pc_validation~execute.
    CONSTANTS: BEGIN OF segment_values,
                 geral   TYPE fb_segment VALUE `GERAL`,
                 ato     TYPE fb_segment VALUE `ATO`,
                 nao_ato TYPE fb_segment VALUE `NAO_ATO`,
               END OF segment_values.

    CHECK is_profit_center-profitcenterforedit+09(01) CA `012`.

    DATA(segment) = SWITCH fb_segment( is_profit_center-profitcenterforedit+09(01) WHEN `0` THEN segment_values-geral
                                                                                   WHEN `1` THEN segment_values-ato
                                                                                   WHEN `2` THEN segment_values-nao_ato ).

    IF is_profit_center-segment <> segment.
      INSERT VALUE #( msgid = `ZFIN`
                      msgty = `E`
                      msgno = 001
                      msgv1 = segment ) INTO TABLE ct_messages.
    ENDIF.
  ENDMETHOD.

This validation logic was implemented within the BAdI execution method, ensuring that the business rule is enforced during the save process.

Result

When the validation is triggered, the system prevents saving and displays an error message to the user.

Fiori error message

Key Insight

The most critical part of this implementation was identifying the correct enhancement point.

Once the correct spot was found, the validation itself was straightforward.

This reinforces that, in many SAP extensibility scenarios, the main challenge is not writing the logic itself, but navigating the framework to identify where that logic should be implemented.

Final Thoughts

This scenario highlights that, in many cases, the real challenge is not the implementation itself, but understanding where and how to implement it within the SAP framework.

👉I would be interested to hear how others approach similar challenges.

entry.title: How to Add a Custom Formatter in SAPUI5: View vs. Controller Approach
entry.content:
Hi Everyone,

Introduction

When building SAPUI5 applications, we often bind data directly from a model to the UI. While simple property binding works well for displaying raw values, there are many scenarios where the displayed value needs additional formatting before it reaches the user. For example, you might want to combine multiple fields into a single sentence, display semantic colors based on a value, or convert technical data into a more user-friendly format.

This is where custom formatters become useful. They allow you to keep formatting logic separate from your XML views, making your application easier to read, maintain, and reuse.

In this blog, we'll explore:

  • What a custom formatter is and why you should use one.
  • Two different ways to integrate an external formatter into your SAPUI5 application:
    • Using core:require directly in the XML View.
    • Importing the formatter through the Controller.
  • A practical example that demonstrates both approaches side by side using real code and application output.
  • When to use each approach, so you can choose the one that best fits your application's structure and development style.

Project Context

While practicing SAPUI5, I wanted to organize my formatting logic outside the XML View instead of writing formatting code directly in bindings. During this exercise, I discovered that SAPUI5 provides more than one way to use external formatter files.

I experimented with both approaches, loading the formatter directly in the XML View using core:require and importing it through the controller. Since both approaches produce the same result but differ in how the formatter is referenced, I thought it would be useful to compare them side by side with a simple practical example.

What is a Custom Formatter, and Why Use One

A custom formatter is a JavaScript function that transforms model data before it is displayed in the UI. It allows you to present data in a more meaningful or user-friendly format without modifying the original data in the model. Since a formatter only affects the displayed value, it supports one-way binding and cannot be used for two-way (editable) bindings.

Some common use cases include:

  • Converting technical values into user-friendly text.
  • Combining multiple model properties into a single display value.
  • Applying semantic states such as Success, Warning, or Error based on a value.
  • Formatting dates, currency, or performing simple calculations before displaying data.

In the following sections, we'll implement these concepts with a practical SAPUI5 example using reusable external formatter files.

Project Setup and Folder Structure

Here's the folder structure used for this example, a simple table of users with their age and salary, where we'll apply one formatter for each column:

project1/                        ← SAPUI5 application
└── webapp/
    ├── controller/               ← Controller logic
    │   ├── App.controller.js
    │   └── View1.controller.js   ← attaches AgeFormatter (controller approach)
    ├── formatter/                ← Reusable external formatter files
    │   ├── AgeFormatter.js       ← used via controller
    │   └── SalaryFormatter.js    ← used via core:require in the view
    ├── model/
    │   ├── models.js
    │   └── users.json            ← sample data
    ├── view/
    │   ├── App.view.xml
    │   └── View1.view.xml        ← table using both formatter approaches
    ├── Component.js
    ├── index.html
    └── manifest.json             ← model & routing configuration

For this example, I created two separate formatter files to demonstrate both approaches independently. One formatter is loaded directly in the XML View using core:require, while the other is imported through the controller. This makes it easier to understand how each approach works without mixing the implementations.

  • AgeFormatter.js – Combines a user's name and age into a readable sentence.
  • SalaryFormatter.js – Maps a salary value to an appropriate semantic state.

Sample Data (users.json)

The application uses a simple JSON model containing user information. The age and salary properties are used by the formatter functions in the following examples.

{
  "users": [
    { "id": 1, "name": "Monkey D. Luffy", "age": 16, "salary": 22000 },
    { "id": 2, "name": "Roronoa Zoro", "age": 21, "salary": 64000 },
    { "id": 3, "name": "Nami", "age": 20, "salary": 2400 },
    { "id": 4, "name": "Usopp", "age": 19, "salary": 2500 },
    { "id": 5, "name": "Sanji", "age": 21, "salary": 7000 },
    { "id": 6, "name": "Tony Tony Chopper", "age": 17, "salary": 6800 },
    { "id": 7, "name": "Nico Robin", "age": 30, "salary": 9200 },
    { "id": 8, "name": "Franky", "age": 36, "salary": 88000 },
    { "id": 9, "name": "Brook", "age": 90, "salary": 33000 },
    { "id": 10, "name": "Jinbe", "age": 46, "salary": 440000 }
  ]
}

Registering the Model (manifest.json)

"oModel": {
    "type": "sap.ui.model.json.JSONModel",
    "uri": "model/users.json"
}

The JSON model is registered in manifest.json, making it available throughout the application using the model name oModel.

Approach 1: Using core:require in the View

This approach loads the formatter module directly in the XML view using the core:require attribute, and gives it an alias you can reference in any binding inside that view. No changes are needed in the controller.

SalaryFormatter.js

sap.ui.define([], function(){
    "use strict";
    return {
         /*
         * This formatter is required directly in the VIEW via core:require
         * (see View1.view.xml) and referenced as 'Salary.salaryState'.
         */
        salaryState: function(salary){
           if (salary >= 10000) {
                return "Success"; // green color
            } else if (salary >= 5000) {
                return "Warning"; // orange color
            } else {
                return "Error";   // red color
            }
        }
    }
})

While implementing this example, I kept the salary-related formatting in a dedicated formatter file instead of writing conditional logic directly in the XML View. This keeps the binding expression clean and makes the formatting logic reusable.

View1.view.xml

<mvc:View controllerName="project1.controller.View1"
    xmlns:mvc="sap.ui.core.mvc"
    xmlns="sap.m"
    xmlns:core="sap.ui.core"
    core:require="{ Salary: 'project1/formatter/SalaryFormatter' }">
    <!-- Approach 1 (view-based):
         core:require loads the SalaryFormatter module and makes it available
         under the alias "Salary" for use anywhere inside this view. -->
    <Page id="page" title="{i18n>title}">
        <Table items="{oModel>/users}">
            <columns>
                <Column><Text text="ID" /></Column>
                <Column><Text text="Name" /></Column>
                <Column><Text text="Age" /></Column>
                <Column><Text text="Salary" /></Column>
                <Column><Text text="Age Classification" /></Column>
            </columns>
            <items>
                <ColumnListItem>
                    <Text text="{oModel>id}" />
                    <Text text="{oModel>name}" />
                    <Text text="{oModel>age}" />

                    <!-- "Salary" here refers to the alias declared in core:require above -->
                    <ObjectStatus text="{oModel>salary}" state="{path:'oModel>salary', formatter:'Salary.salaryState'}" />
                </ColumnListItem>
            </items>
        </Table>
    </Page>
</mvc:View>

One thing I found convenient about this approach is that everything required by the formatter stays inside the XML View. Since the formatter is only used in this view, there was no need to modify the controller. This also keeps the formatter dependency close to where it is actually used.

Approach 2: Attaching the Formatter in the Controller

While exploring formatter implementations, I also tried importing the formatter through the controller. In this approach, the formatter is loaded as a controller dependency and then exposed to the XML View through the formatter property.

AgeFormatter.js

sap.ui.define([], function () {
    "use strict";

    return {
        // Classifies a person's age into a category and returns a readable sentence.
        ageClassification: function (sName, sAge) {
            var nAge = Number(sAge);
            var sCategory;

            if (nAge < 13) {
                sCategory = "Child";
            } else if (nAge < 18) {
                sCategory = "Youth";
            } else if (nAge < 60) {
                sCategory = "Adult";
            } else {
                sCategory = "Senior Citizen";
            }

            // Pick "a" or "an" based on whether the category starts with a vowel sound
            var sArticle = /^[aeiou]/i.test(sCategory) ? "an" : "a";

            return `${sName} is ${sArticle} ${sCategory}`;
        }
    };
});

View1.controller.js

sap.ui.define([
    "sap/ui/core/mvc/Controller",
    // Import the formatter module here and attach it to the controller
    "project1/formatter/AgeFormatter"
], (Controller, AgeFormatter) => {
    "use strict";
    return Controller.extend("project1.controller.View1", {

        // Attaching the module as "formatter" is what makes
        // ".formatter.ageClassification" resolvable from the view
        formatter: AgeFormatter,

        onInit() {
        }
    });
});
View1.view.xml (relevant part)
<!-- The leading dot means "look this up on the controller instance",
     i.e. this.formatter.ageClassification -->
<Text text="{parts:[{path:'oModel>name'}, {path:'oModel>age'}], formatter:'.formatter.ageClassification'}" />

Only the binding expression changes in the XML View. The remaining XML structure stays exactly the same as in the previous approach, so only the relevant binding is shown below.

Figure 1: XML View showing where each formatter approach is used

After implementing both approaches, I noticed that the application behavior and output remain exactly the same. The only difference is how the formatter module is made available to the XML View. This observation helped me understand that the choice is primarily about code organization rather than functionality.

Putting Both Together

Here's the same app with both formatters wired up, salary status via the view-based approach, age classification via the controller-based approach, running against a simple JSON model:

Figure 2: Output showing salary status and age classification for all users

Both approaches produce correct, working output. The difference is purely in where the dependency is declared and how it's referenced.

View vs. Controller Approach

Both approaches ultimately achieve the same goal. They allow you to keep formatting logic outside the XML View and produce the same output. After implementing both in the same application, I noticed that the primary difference isn't what they do, but where the formatter dependency is managed.

With the View approach (core:require), the formatter is imported directly into the XML View. This makes the view self-contained because anyone reading the XML can immediately see which formatter modules it depends on. I found this approach straightforward when the formatter is only used within a single view.

With the Controller approach, the formatter is imported as a controller dependency and then exposed to the XML View through the formatter property. This keeps all module imports together inside the controller, which can make the XML slightly cleaner. I found this approach useful because the controller becomes the single place responsible for managing its dependencies.

Another thing I observed is that the formatter implementation itself doesn't change. The same formatter function can be used with either approach. Only the way SAPUI5 resolves and accesses that function is different.

From a functionality perspective, both approaches produce identical results. The choice mainly comes down to how you prefer to organize your application and how your project structures reusable modules.

Conclusion

While building this example, I realized that both approaches ultimately achieve the same result. The difference lies in how the formatter dependency is organized rather than how the formatter behaves. Implementing and comparing both approaches side by side gave me a better understanding of how SAPUI5 resolves formatter functions, and I hope this practical comparison helps you make the right choice for your own applications.

Thank you for reading! If you've used a different approach or have additional insights, I'd love to hear about them in the comments.

entry.title: Fixing Sort, Group & Reset in Fiori Elements with CAP CDS What's Not Standard and What's Needed
entry.content:
The Problem

In a SAP CAP Fiori Elements List Report or Object Page application, you might find that Sort, Group, and Reset personalization features do not work out of the box, especially when your entity uses navigation properties (associations) as display columns.

Symptoms

  • Clicking Sort on a column throws a backend error or does nothing
  • Group dialog shows columns that cannot be grouped, causing OData errors
  • Reset or Restore button does not work, or personalization state is lost on page refresh
  • Search works but sort and group personalization is broken

Example Scenario

Consider an entity that displays details from an associated entity:

entity OrderItems {
  key order   : Association to Orders;
  key product : Association to Products;
}

Your @ui.LineItem annotation displays navigation properties:

annotate MyService.OrderItems with @(UI: {
  LineItem: [
    { Value: product_ID },          // flat key - works fine
    { Value: product.name },        // navigation property - breaks sort
    { Value: product.category },    // navigation property - breaks sort
    { Value: order.customerName }   // navigation property - breaks sort
  ]
});

Fiori Elements will show all these columns in the Sort and Group personalization dialog. But the OData backend cannot sort on navigation properties like product.name. Attempting to do so results in a runtime error.

What is Standard vs. What You Need to Add

Standard (works out of the box)

Feature Works For

SortingFlat or direct properties of the entity (e.g., product_ID, order_ID)
GroupingFlat or direct properties
Table rendering@ui.LineItem, @ui.PresentationVariant annotations
Search@CDS.search annotation on the entity

NOT Standard - You Must Configure These

The following three fixes are required when your table displays navigation properties.

Fix 1: SortRestrictions for Navigation Properties

When your @ui.LineItem displays columns from associations, Fiori Elements will offer these in the Sort and Group dialog by default. But the OData backend cannot sort on navigation properties.

The Fix

Add Capabilities.SortRestrictions.NonSortableProperties to exclude navigation properties from sort and group:

annotate MyService.OrderItems with @(
  Capabilities: {
    SortRestrictions: {
      NonSortableProperties: [
        product_ID,
        product.name,
        product.category,
        product.description,
        order.customerName,
        order.status
      ]
    }
  }
);

What This Does

  • Tells Fiori Elements not to offer these columns in the Sort or Group personalization dialog
  • Prevents runtime OData errors when users try to sort
  • The columns still display in the table. They just cannot be sorted or grouped.

When to Use

  • Any time your @ui.LineItem references a navigation property (e.g., product.name, order.type.description)
  • Deep navigation paths like order.statusRef.name

Fix 2: data-sap-ui-flexibility-services for Personalization Persistence

By default, Fiori Elements personalization (sort, group, filter variants) requires a flexibility service to store the user's settings. Without it, the Reset button may not work and personalization state is lost on refresh.

The Fix

Add the following attributes to your app's index.html bootstrap script:

<script
    id="sap-ui-bootstrap"
    src="https://sapui5.hana.ondemand.com/resources/sap-ui-core.js"
    crossorigin="anonymous"
    data-sap-ui-theme="sap_horizon"
    data-sap-ui-resourceroots='{
        "com.mycompany.myapp": "./"
    }'
    data-sap-ui-oninit="module:sap/ui/core/ComponentSupport"
    data-sap-ui-compatVersion="edge"
    data-sap-ui-async="true"
    data-sap-ui-frameOptions="trusted"
    data-sap-ui-flexibility-services='[{"connector": "SessionStorageConnector"}]'
></script>

Key Attributes

Attribute Purpose

crossorigin="anonymous"Required for proper CORS handling of the UI5 library resources
data-sap-ui-flexibility-servicesConfigures where personalization state is stored
SessionStorageConnectorStores personalization in browser session storage (development or standalone)

Production vs. Development

  • Development or Standalone: Use SessionStorageConnector. It stores personalization in browser session storage.
  • Production on BTP Launchpad: The SAPUI5 Flexibility Service handles this automatically via the Launchpad shell. You typically do not need this attribute when deployed to a managed Launchpad.
  • Standalone production apps: Use SessionStorageConnector or configure a persistence backend.

Fix 3: @ui.HiddenFilter for Non-Filterable Fields

If your entity exposes fields via associations that should not appear in the filter bar's "Adapt Filters" dialog:

annotate MyService.Products with {
  description  @UI.HiddenFilter;
  category     @UI.HiddenFilter;
  manufacturer @UI.HiddenFilter;
  weight       @UI.HiddenFilter;
}

What This Does

  • Hides these fields from the "Adapt Filters" dialog in the List Report
  • Prevents users from trying to filter on fields that may not be efficiently queryable
  • The fields still display in tables. They are just not offered as filter options.

Complete Example

Here is a complete annotation setup for an entity with navigation properties:

// Entity definition with associations
entity OrderItems {
  key order   : Association to Orders;
  key product : Association to Products;
      quantity : Integer;
}

// Annotations
annotate MyService.OrderItems with @(
  // Prevent sort and group on navigation properties
  Capabilities: {
    SortRestrictions: {
      NonSortableProperties: [
        product_ID,
        product.name,
        product.category,
        product.description,
        order.customerName
      ]
    }
  },
  UI: {
    PresentationVariant: { Visualizations: ['@UI.LineItem'] },
    LineItem: [
      { Value: product_ID,          ![@UI.Importance]: #High },
      { Value: product.name,        ![@UI.Importance]: #High },
      { Value: product.category,    ![@UI.Importance]: #Medium },
      { Value: order.customerName,  ![@UI.Importance]: #Medium },
      { Value: quantity }
    ]
  }
);

// Hide non-filterable fields from the Adapt Filters dialog
annotate MyService.Products with {
  description  @UI.HiddenFilter;
  category     @UI.HiddenFilter;
  manufacturer @UI.HiddenFilter;
}

Summary

Feature Standard? What to Add Where

Sort on flat propertiesYesNothingN/A
Sort on navigation propertiesNo, failsSortRestrictions.NonSortablePropertiesCDS annotations
Group on flat propertiesYesNothingN/A
Group on navigation propertiesNo, failsSortRestrictions.NonSortablePropertiesCDS annotations
Reset personalizationMay not workdata-sap-ui-flexibility-servicesindex.html
Personalization persistenceLost on refreshSessionStorageConnectorindex.html
Hide fields from filter dialogShows all@ui.HiddenFilterCDS annotations
CORS for UI5 resourcesMay warncrossorigin="anonymous"index.html

Key Takeaway

Fiori Elements is powerful but assumes your OData entity is flat. When you use CAP associations and display navigation properties in tables, you must explicitly tell the framework which columns are non-sortable and non-groupable. Without this, the personalization dialogs offer options that crash at runtime.

The three things to always check:

  1. SortRestrictions for every navigation property in your @ui.LineItem
  2. flexibility-services in your index.html for personalization to work
  3. @ui.HiddenFilter for fields that should not be in the filter dialog

References

  • CAP CDS Annotations: Capabilities
  • Fiori Elements: Configuring Tables
  • SAPUI5 Flexibility Services

entry.title: How To Create Value Help In CAPM.
entry.content:
Hi Everyone,

In this blog, I'll walk you through one of the easiest ways to implement Value Help (F4 Help) in a SAP CAPM application.

While developing SAP Fiori applications, we often have fields such as Category, Department, Country, Employee, Supplier, and many others where users should select values instead of typing them manually. If users enter values manually, there is always a chance of entering incorrect or inconsistent data.

To solve this problem, SAP provides Value Help (F4 Help), which allows users to choose values from a predefined list. This not only improves the user experience but also ensures that only valid data is saved.

In this blog, I'll demonstrate how to create a simple Value Help for the Category field by using a lookup entity, exposing it through a CAP service, and configuring the required CDS annotations.

To Demonstrate this I have Created a simple CAPM application, folder structure of this Looks like this below image



Step 1 – Create Database Entities

First, open the db folder and create a schema.cds file.

In this example, I created two entities:

  • Products
  • Categories

The Products entity stores the product details, while the Categories entity acts as the lookup table that provides the values for the Value Help.

Below is the content of my schema.cds file.

namespace demo.valuehelp;

entity Products {
  key ID       : UUID;
  name         : String(100);
  category     : String(10);
  price        : Decimal(10,2);
}

entity Categories {
  key code     : String(10);
  description  : String(50);
}

Step 2 – Create Sample Data

Next, inside the db/data folder, I created two CSV files.

  • demo.valuehelp-Categories.csv
  • demo.valuehelp-Products.csv

The Categories CSV file contains the values that will be displayed in the Value Help dialog.

Below is the content of the Categories CSV file.

code,description
ELEC,Electronics
FURN,Furniture
BOOK,Books

The Products CSV file contains sample product records.

ID,name,category,price
3b5a1e2a-1111-4a1a-9a1a-000000000001,Laptop,ELEC,55000.00
3b5a1e2a-1111-4a1a-9a1a-000000000002,Office Chair,FURN,4500.00
3b5a1e2a-1111-4a1a-9a1a-000000000003,Notebook Set,BOOK,250.00

Step 3 – Create the Service

Next, create a service.cds file inside the srv folder.

In this file, I exposed both entities as projections so that they are available through OData services.

Below is the content of my service.cds file.

using demo.valuehelp as db from '../db/schema';

service CatalogService {
  entity Products   as projection on db.Products;
  entity Categories as projection on db.Categories;
}

Step 4 – Configure Value Help

After exposing the required entities in the service, I created a Fiori application inside the app folder by selecting New Project from Template in SAP Business Application Studio. I chose my local CAP application as the data source, and the project automatically generated the annotations.cds file.

In this file, I added the required UI annotations and configured the @Common.ValueList annotation for the Category field. The CollectionPath property points to the Categories entity, while LocalDataProperty and ValueListProperty map the selected value from the Value Help dialog back to the Product entity.

One thing I learned during this implementation is that even a small typo in an annotation or entity name can prevent the Value Help from working, while no clear error is shown in the UI. I found it helpful to verify the service metadata and ensure that all entity and property names matched exactly before troubleshooting further. This saved me a significant amount of debugging time.

Below is the content of my annotations.cds file.

using CatalogService as service from '../../srv/service';

annotate service.Products with @(
  UI.SelectionFields: [ category, name ],
  UI.LineItem: [
    { Value: name },
    { Value: category },
    { Value: price }
  ]
) {
  category @Common.ValueList : {
    Label          : 'Category',
    CollectionPath : 'Categories',
    Parameters     : [
      { $Type: 'Common.ValueListParameterInOut', LocalDataProperty: category, ValueListProperty: 'code' },
      { $Type: 'Common.ValueListParameterDisplayOnly', ValueListProperty: 'description' }
    ]
  };
};

Step 5 – Run the Application

After completing the above configuration, open the terminal and execute the following command.

cds watch

Once the application starts successfully, open the generated Fiori application.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 


here click on the URL which is having index.html
In this application I created value Help for a Selection field I mean a Search field.
Click On the Value Help Icon 
Here is the Image How My Value Help is looking

How Does It Work?

Let's quickly understand what happens behind the scenes.

  • The Categories entity stores all valid category values.
  • The Products entity contains the Category field.
  • The service.cds file exposes both entities.
  • The annotations.cds file connects the Product Category field with the Categories entity by using the @Common.ValueList annotation.
  • When the application runs, SAP Fiori automatically recognizes the annotation and displays the Value Help dialog.

Because of this configuration, users don't have to type category values manually, reducing data entry mistakes and improving consistency.

Conclusion

In this blog, we learned how to implement Value Help in SAP CAPM by using a lookup entity, service projections, and CDS annotations.

We created the required database entities, loaded sample data using CSV files, exposed the entities through a CAP service, and configured the @Common.ValueList annotation to display the Value Help dialog in the SAP Fiori application.

This approach is simple, reusable, and can be applied to many business scenarios such as Customers, Suppliers, Departments, Countries, Employees, or any other master data.

I hope this blog helps you understand how Value Help works in SAP CAPM.

Thank you for reading!
